jokes.text: What's your secret? I'm bisexual. Oh, you mean the dessert!
jokes.setup: Someone asks about Amy's secret for making Baked Alaska
jokes.punchline: Amy misinterprets as asking about her personal life, then realizes they meant the dessert
jokes.explanation: Clean misdirection with good economy. The misunderstanding feels natural for Amy's character.

jokes.text: Show Fry and Leela your painting so they can compliment it.
jokes.setup: Mother tells child to show off painting
jokes.punchline: Explicitly states the expectation that they must compliment it
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing pushy parent behavior with direct honesty about expectations.

jokes.text: You really captured your mommy's unusually shaped hair, and your daddy's pukey color.
jokes.setup: Fry is forced to compliment a child's painting
jokes.punchline: His 'compliments' are actually insults about the parents' appearance
jokes.explanation: Excellent character moment - Fry's obliviousness leads to accidentally brutal honesty while trying to be nice.

jokes.text: You know, I always wanted to be an artist, and I-- That's so interesting. Let's talk about something else.
jokes.setup: Fry starts to share personal information about his artistic dreams
jokes.punchline: Amy's mother immediately shuts him down with fake politeness
jokes.explanation: Sharp social awkwardness with perfect timing. The abrupt dismissal is both rude and funny.

jokes.text: I'm so bored, I wanna punch myself in the face. I'll do it for ya. Go ahead. Make me. Eh, I'm too bored.
jokes.setup: Bender expresses extreme boredom through self-violence threat
jokes.punchline: The escalation deflates when Bender is too bored to follow through
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bender character moment - the buildup and deflation matches his lazy personality perfectly.

jokes.text: Bo-ring.
jokes.setup: Radio announces war roundup which sounds potentially exciting
jokes.punchline: Bender finds even war news boring
jokes.explanation: Simple character beat. Works for Bender but not particularly clever or memorable.

jokes.text: What are those, numbers? Two... 'Two'? I'm almost positive that's a number!
jokes.setup: Numbers are broadcast over radio
jokes.punchline: Fry acts like he's making a groundbreaking discovery that 'two' is a number
jokes.explanation: Classic Fry stupidity - treating basic knowledge as revelation. His confidence makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Help, help, help, help, help, help, help, help, help, help. The radio's saying numbers at me!
jokes.setup: Numbers are being broadcast on radio
jokes.punchline: Bender panics as if the numbers are attacking him personally
jokes.explanation: Excellent setup for Bender's number phobia. The personification of numbers 'saying at me' is wonderfully absurd.

jokes.text: It's actually a real thing. Look it up.
jokes.setup: Characters discuss mysterious numbers stations
jokes.punchline: Breaking fourth wall to tell audience this is a real phenomenon
jokes.explanation: Mild meta humor. Educational but not particularly funny - more of an easter egg for knowledgeable viewers.

jokes.text: I'm not touching your antenna.
jokes.setup: Bender wants the radio turned off
jokes.punchline: Leela's refusal has sexual undertones given robot anatomy
jokes.explanation: Mild innuendo joke. Works but is fairly predictable given the setup.

jokes.text: Not all numbers. I-I love zero and one. But two? And those other guys? Where does it all end?
jokes.setup: Bender is asked if he's scared of numbers
jokes.punchline: He's fine with binary but terrified of the infinite progression beyond
jokes.explanation: Brilliant robot-specific phobia. Makes perfect sense for a binary being to fear higher math while loving 0 and 1.

jokes.text: Leela, have I ever told you how I always wanted to be an artist? Yes, it's one of the most recent things you said.
jokes.setup: Fry asks if he's mentioned wanting to be an artist
jokes.punchline: Leela deadpans that he literally just said it minutes ago
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback timing and Leela's dry delivery. Shows both Fry's obliviousness and Leela's patience.

jokes.text: Remember the pain I predicted Professor would live in the last 10 years of his life? Well, they've discovered a miracle drug that'll make it 20!
jokes.setup: Zoidberg mentions a miracle drug discovery
jokes.punchline: The 'miracle' is extending suffering, not ending it
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark humor with perfect misdirection. The setup makes you expect good news, delivering the opposite.

jokes.text: These scratchy physical pajamas.
jokes.setup: Professor complains about physical existence
jokes.punchline: Includes pajama texture as part of existential suffering
jokes.explanation: Good character detail. The specificity of 'scratchy physical pajamas' among cosmic complaints is nicely absurd.

jokes.text: You mean Bender's antenna is magic? Eh, that's what the fembots say.
jokes.setup: Someone suggests Bender's antenna has magical properties
jokes.punchline: Bender implies sexual prowess with female robots
jokes.explanation: Classic Bender braggart moment with innuendo. Predictable but fits character perfectly.

jokes.text: It's called 'paint-by-numbers.' Oh, Lord.
jokes.setup: Fry excitedly describes futuristic art technology
jokes.punchline: It's just basic paint-by-numbers, Leela's reaction shows the anticlimax
jokes.explanation: Good setup/payoff with Fry's enthusiasm for something mundane. Leela's reaction sells the joke.

jokes.text: They're exquisite! You really stayed in the lines.
jokes.setup: Amy looks at Fry's paint-by-number artwork
jokes.punchline: Her praise is for the most basic skill possible in art
jokes.explanation: Nice backhanded compliment. Praising line-staying as artistic achievement is perfectly condescending.

jokes.text: What are you people talking about? Fry's paintings are crap. Ow! I mean, the foot pain makes me see how beautiful I'm saying they are.
jokes.setup: Amy starts to give honest criticism of Fry's art
jokes.punchline: Gets hurt and has to backtrack with nonsensical excuse
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy leads to awkward social recovery. The excuse logic is wonderfully convoluted.

jokes.text: Fry, you foolcompoop! What are you doing to those beautiful numbers? Painting over them to depict imperfect physical objects?
jokes.setup: Professor sees Fry's paint-by-numbers work
jokes.punchline: He's horrified that Fry covered up the numbers with actual painting
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Professor's mathematical obsession. 'Foolcompoop' is a nice made-up insult.

jokes.text: Schmeureka!
jokes.setup: Professor has a realization
jokes.punchline: Combines 'schmuck' with 'eureka' for a Yiddish-scientific exclamation
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay that fits Professor's character. Single word packs both personality and meaning efficiently.

jokes.text: Every time we find a mysterious diagram, you want to build it. Well, I'm tired of building things.
jokes.setup: Characters debate whether to build mysterious device
jokes.punchline: Professor acknowledges this is their standard plot pattern
jokes.explanation: Nice meta-commentary on the show's recurring plot device. Self-aware without being too heavy-handed.

jokes.text: If you need me, I'll be in the bathroom. Exquisite!
jokes.setup: Professor goes to contemplate abstract beauty
jokes.punchline: His appreciation continues even in the bathroom
jokes.explanation: Bathroom humor elevated by Professor's continued aesthetic appreciation. The callback to his catchphrase works well.

jokes.text: I wonder if it's some kind of advanced spaceship! Well, I'm not quite done. It might turn out to be a kitten.
jokes.setup: Fry speculates about the mysterious device being advanced technology
jokes.punchline: Reveals he's just following paint-by-numbers and doesn't know what he's making
jokes.explanation: Perfect Fry moment - completely missing the point while being accidentally profound. The kitten possibility is wonderfully random.

jokes.text: That's no kitten. Rats. I'm gonna go paint a kitten.
jokes.setup: Fry realizes the device isn't a kitten
jokes.punchline: His solution is to go paint an actual kitten instead
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up to previous joke. Fry's disappointed but practical response fits his character.

jokes.text: Great artists don't let badness stand in the way of greatness. And Zoidberg will prove... Why did you mutter that last part? Because I wasn't that sure.
jokes.setup: Zoidberg gives confident artistic advice
jokes.punchline: Immediately undermines himself by admitting uncertainty
jokes.explanation: Perfect Zoidberg character beat - starts confident then deflates. The honest follow-up makes it endearing rather than just pathetic.

jokes.text: Could you sign this book? Sure. Wait, I didn't write this. It's my autobiography. You're Bender? The Bender? I'm a huge fan.
jokes.setup: Bender asks celebrity to sign a book
jokes.punchline: It's his own autobiography, and she's a fan of a different Bender
jokes.explanation: Good case of mistaken identity with nice escalation. Bender's ego getting deflated works well.

jokes.text: You're not the extreme boogie boarder? How do people tell you apart? Different serial numbers.
jokes.setup: Confusion about which Bender this is
jokes.punchline: The only distinguishing feature is serial numbers - highlighting robot anonymity
jokes.explanation: Nice robot-specific humor about identity and mass production. The mundane answer to an identity crisis is funny.

jokes.text: Hey, could we talk about me for a minute?! I don't know. Could you buy a copy of my book?
jokes.setup: Bender desperately tries to redirect attention to himself
jokes.punchline: McKellar only engages if there's a financial transaction involved
jokes.explanation: Good satire of celebrity book signings and commercial interactions. Both characters' motivations are clear and selfish.

jokes.text: And a fear of irrational numbers! Don't be afraid. If you're even half as great as your book jacket says you are-- I'm not! Nobody is!
jokes.setup: McKellar tries to build Bender's confidence
jokes.punchline: Her self-deprecation escalates to include all of humanity
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of self-doubt. The universal application makes it funnier than just personal insecurity.

jokes.text: Why are you reading my mail, Hermes? Also, I don't remember entering any art contest. I entered you! Zoidberg.
jokes.setup: Fry questions Hermes about reading his mail and contest entry
jokes.punchline: Zoidberg admits to entering Fry without permission, parenthetical delivery
jokes.explanation: Nice reveal of Zoidberg's meddling. The casual 'Zoidberg.' as explanation is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: I've completed the mystery machine! I'm blind! What does it do? Can it kill at least one human? Maybe.
jokes.setup: Professor reveals completed device
jokes.punchline: Amy immediately focuses on its killing potential, Professor's casual 'maybe'
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor with Amy's priorities and Professor's nonchalant response to potential murder.

jokes.text: after great effort and expense-- / Expense?!
jokes.setup: Professor listing his accomplishments
jokes.punchline: Hermes' alarmed focus on financial aspect
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment for Hermes' financial concerns overriding everything else.

jokes.text: Finished installin' the new plate glass wind-er. Thank you! We'll sweep him off the sidewalk later.
jokes.setup: Window installer has just finished work
jokes.punchline: Someone presumably crashed through it immediately, will be cleaned up later
jokes.explanation: Classic visual gag setup - immediate destruction of newly installed item. The casual cleanup attitude adds to the humor.

jokes.text: My decimal representation is five 10s plus five ones. But you can call me five times 11. Or you can call me 9 plus 10 plus 11 plus 12 plus 13. Or you can call me six-squared plus three-squared plus three-squared plus one-squared. But ya doesn't have to call me 55!
jokes.setup: Number 55 introduces himself with mathematical alternatives
jokes.punchline: Escalating mathematical representations ending with reference to classic song
jokes.explanation: Brilliant mathematical wordplay referencing classic song structure. The escalation of equivalent expressions is both educational and funny.

jokes.text: I'm gonna call you 55.
jokes.setup: 55's elaborate introduction with many naming options
jokes.punchline: Fry's simple, practical choice
jokes.explanation: Perfect punctuation to the previous joke. Fry's simple-minded response deflates the mathematical complexity beautifully.

jokes.text: I'm gonna call you 55.
jokes.setup: 55's elaborate list of alternative names
jokes.punchline: Leela's practical dismissal of all options
jokes.explanation: Perfect straight-man response with excellent economy. Classic Leela practicality.

jokes.text: Every number is interesting! Otherwise, there'd be a smallest uninteresting number, and that would make it interesting.
jokes.setup: Question about interesting numbers
jokes.punchline: Paradox about uninteresting numbers
jokes.explanation: Clever mathematical paradox presented as character dialogue. Shows the writers' intelligence while staying funny.

jokes.text: Every number is interesting! Otherwise, there'd be a smallest uninteresting number, and that would make it interesting.
jokes.setup: Characters ask about interesting numbers
jokes.punchline: Paradoxical logic that all numbers must be interesting
jokes.explanation: Clever mathematical paradox presented as casual conversation. The logic is sound and genuinely mind-bending.

jokes.text: That's little 3/7ths, the fractional child of three and seven. What about 7/3rds? Is that also their child? Their illegitimate child.
jokes.setup: Introduction to fractional family relationships
jokes.punchline: 7/3 is the 'illegitimate' child because it's improper fraction
jokes.explanation: Brilliant mathematical anthropomorphization. The improper fraction being 'illegitimate' is mathematically clever and socially funny.

jokes.text: You never grow old and senile and forgetful and senile and forgetful and senile and...
jokes.setup: Professor asks if numbers age
jokes.punchline: Demonstrates his own senility by repeating himself while denying it
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Professor exemplifies what he's talking about. The irony is both obvious and well-executed.

jokes.text: You mean you never age? You never grow old and senile and forgetful and senile and forgetful and senile and...
jokes.setup: Question about numbers being eternal
jokes.punchline: Professor's repetitive demonstration of his own senility
jokes.explanation: Excellent self-demonstrating humor where the Professor proves his point through his own repetitive behavior.

jokes.text: What's almost four? Three?
jokes.setup: Someone says 'almost four'
jokes.punchline: Fry's uncertain grasp of number proximity
jokes.explanation: Perfect Fry moment - his mathematical confusion presented as genuine uncertainty about basic concepts.

jokes.text: Ya know, one through 12 are my friends. I don't appreciate these racist caricatures.
jokes.setup: Looking at a clock face
jokes.punchline: Clock positions are seen as racist representations of numbers
jokes.explanation: Clever anthropomorphization of numbers having opinions about their representation. Social commentary through mathematical lens.

jokes.text: Oh, don't be sad. You won't.
jokes.setup: Fry wanting Professor at art contest in case he wins
jokes.punchline: Professor's blunt prediction of failure
jokes.explanation: Perfect Professor moment - brutal honesty delivered as comfort. The economy is flawless and the character integration is spot-on.

jokes.text: Oh, don't be sad. You won't.
jokes.setup: Fry wants Professor to come in case he wins a prize
jokes.punchline: Professor casually crushes Fry's hopes while comforting him
jokes.explanation: Perfect Professor moment - brutal honesty delivered as comfort. The economy and timing are excellent.

jokes.text: I can't think of any actual difference. Just felt like somebody should say that.
jokes.setup: Hermes says it won't be the same without Professor
jokes.punchline: Admits there's no real difference, was just following social convention
jokes.explanation: Good meta-commentary on empty social platitudes. Hermes's bureaucratic honesty makes it funnier.

jokes.text: You two-loving fools!
jokes.setup: Bender is angry about letting Professor stay
jokes.punchline: Insults them using mathematical terminology reflecting his number phobia
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback to Bender's established phobia. Using 'two-loving' as an insult is both character-specific and clever.

jokes.text: You escaped?! That's mathematically impossible!
jokes.setup: Characters return from Numberland
jokes.punchline: Bender's concern framed in mathematical terms
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Bender's mathematical anxiety while maintaining his personality.

jokes.text: What else is there? / Oh, all kinds of junk. E, pi, i.
jokes.setup: Question about what numbers exist beyond whole numbers and fractions
jokes.punchline: Dismissive description of fundamental mathematical constants as 'junk'
jokes.explanation: Good mathematical humor showing the dismissive attitude toward irrational and complex numbers.

jokes.text: Don't talk like that! We don't believe in the imaginary one. Except for some of the more... rural numbers.
jokes.setup: Discussion of mathematical concepts like imaginary numbers
jokes.punchline: Imaginary numbers are treated like controversial beliefs, rural numbers are more accepting
jokes.explanation: Brilliant satire of religious/political divisions applied to mathematics. The rural/urban divide in number society is inspired.

jokes.text: 'Understanding'? I don't understand.
jokes.setup: Professor asks about gaining understanding through math
jokes.punchline: Number doesn't understand the concept of understanding
jokes.explanation: Clean paradox with perfect economy. The circular logic of not understanding understanding is philosophically funny.

jokes.text: How many stars to give a Keanu Reeves movie. Yo.
jokes.setup: Professor explains practical uses of numbers
jokes.punchline: Keanu Reeves randomly appears and acknowledges the reference
jokes.explanation: Random celebrity cameo with mild self-deprecation. The 'Yo' is perfectly Keanu but the joke is fairly simple.

jokes.text: You use numbers? What are we, your servants?! Well, I suppose you are. Servants?!
jokes.setup: Numbers learn they're used to describe things
jokes.punchline: Professor's honest confirmation of their servant status causes outrage
jokes.explanation: Good escalation from discovery to outrage. Professor's matter-of-fact confirmation adds to the conflict perfectly.

jokes.text: Help! 9-1-1! Sorry, wrong number.
jokes.setup: Professor calls for emergency help
jokes.punchline: 911 responds literally as a number, not emergency service
jokes.explanation: Excellent literal interpretation gag. The concept of 911 being just another number in Numberland is clever and well-executed.

jokes.text: If you're young, then I'm divisible by seven.
jokes.setup: Number claims to be too young to be 'zeroed'
jokes.punchline: Professor uses mathematical impossibility to express disbelief
jokes.explanation: Brilliant mathematical equivalent of 'when pigs fly.' Shows Professor adapting to mathematical realm while maintaining his personality.

jokes.text: If you're young, then I'm divisible by seven.
jokes.setup: Argument about age
jokes.punchline: Mathematical property used as sarcastic retort
jokes.explanation: Creative use of mathematical properties as character dialogue. Shows how numbers would actually 'think' and argue.

jokes.text: My name is Georg Cantor. Georg Cantor?! The discoverer of the hierarchy of infinities? Ja, ja. I discovered a lot of stuff.
jokes.setup: Introduction to famous mathematician
jokes.punchline: Cantor's modest response to his impressive reputation
jokes.explanation: Nice understatement from historical figure. The casual dismissal of major mathematical discoveries is endearing.

jokes.text: I needed only to prove a transmitter could exist. And then build it out of pencils and paper.
jokes.setup: Question about building transmitter in abstract realm
jokes.punchline: Mathematical proof made physical reality possible with simple materials
jokes.explanation: Clever blend of abstract mathematics and practical engineering. The simplicity of the materials contrasts nicely with the complexity of the concept.

jokes.text: I know not which of us is the stupider professor. You.
jokes.setup: Cantor wonders which professor is more stupid
jokes.punchline: Professor immediately answers, implicating himself
jokes.explanation: Perfect Professor moment - his quick, honest self-assessment is both stupid and self-aware, creating perfect irony.

jokes.text: P... R... O... Prostitute? Wait, F? Proftitute? E... S... S... Oh, of course! Professtitute! No, wait. O... R... Professor!
jokes.setup: Bender decodes Morse code message
jokes.punchline: Creates increasingly ridiculous word combinations before getting the right answer
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation of mispronunciation. 'Professtitute' is a particularly inspired combination that's both funny and memorable.

jokes.text: Professtitute! No, wait. O... R... Professor!
jokes.setup: Bender decoding Morse code message
jokes.punchline: Accidentally creating inappropriate word before getting it right
jokes.explanation: Simple wordplay that fits Bender's character. The accidental profanity works with his personality.

jokes.text: In your case only, I'd advise you to stay the heck away from math. Thanks, Danica. I'll never face my fears again. So long, courage!
jokes.setup: Bender imagines getting advice from Danica McKellar
jokes.punchline: Takes the worst possible advice and enthusiastically abandons courage
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bender character moment - takes advice that confirms his cowardice and celebrates it. The dramatic 'So long, courage!' is perfectly over the top.

jokes.text: These are really good. I mean, I'd hang that in my second-best mansion.
jokes.setup: Amy complimenting art
jokes.punchline: Revealing her wealth through casual mansion reference
jokes.explanation: Good character work showing Amy's casual wealth. The 'second-best' qualifier makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Wow, better than the one for winning?
jokes.setup: Being told participation is the best prize
jokes.punchline: Fry's literal interpretation missing the empty platitude
jokes.explanation: Perfect Fry moment taking platitude literally and exposing its logical flaw.

jokes.text: Maybe. But, just being included is the best prize of all. Wow, better than the one for winning?
jokes.setup: Amy gives standard consolation about participation being the real prize
jokes.punchline: Fry takes it literally and questions the logic
jokes.explanation: Good deconstruction of empty consolation platitudes. Fry's literal interpretation exposes the logical flaw in feel-good sayings.

jokes.text: Oh, I'm not allowed to have pets. They always die. I'm on the Humane Society's 'No Adopt' list.
jokes.setup: Judge asks where Fry's pet is
jokes.punchline: Fry reveals his tragic history with pet ownership
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor with callback potential to Fry's established pet troubles. The institutional response makes it funnier.

jokes.text: But this contest is for paintings by animals. It is? Bad Fry, bad!
jokes.setup: Contest rules are revealed
jokes.punchline: Judge treats Fry like a misbehaving pet
jokes.explanation: Simple role reversal humor. The pet training response to human contestant is mildly amusing but predictable.

jokes.text: Second place. I won second place! Yeah, in a contest for animals. And I beat all of them. Well, except Vincent Van Goat.
jokes.setup: Fry's enthusiasm about second place in animal contest
jokes.punchline: Vincent Van Goat as the winner
jokes.explanation: Good pun and character moment showing Fry's ability to find the positive. Vincent Van Goat is a solid reference.

jokes.text: And I beat all of them. Well, except Vincent Van Goat.
jokes.setup: Fry celebrates beating animals in art contest
jokes.punchline: Lost to artist goat with pun name
jokes.explanation: Standard pun on famous artist name. Works but is fairly predictable. The concept of artistic animals is mildly amusing.

jokes.text: Yeah, wait till you see my ass!
jokes.setup: Cantor expresses amazement at meeting a robot
jokes.punchline: Bender immediately makes crude reference to his rear end
jokes.explanation: Classic Bender crude humor. In character but not particularly clever - relies on shock value rather than wit.

jokes.text: Falsch! Order! Order! Look, you, I can prove it. I doubt that. But if you can, we shall free you. Really? Maybe.
jokes.setup: Trial proceeding with dramatic stakes
jokes.punchline: Judge's casual 'maybe' deflates the dramatic promise
jokes.explanation: Standard deflation humor. The buildup and casual dismissal works but isn't particularly memorable or clever.

jokes.text: It's a freak! You think so only because it's not any of your children!
jokes.setup: Numbers react to Cantor's diagonal argument
jokes.punchline: Cantor explains their xenophobic reaction to mathematical proof
jokes.explanation: Clever commentary on how mathematical discoveries can seem threatening. The family metaphor makes abstract concepts relatable.

jokes.text: Stand back, fellow math lovers, and watch as I break up Googol, right between O's.
jokes.setup: Bender confronts the large number Googol
jokes.punchline: Plans to literally break up the word between its repeated letters
jokes.explanation: Good wordplay on the spelling of 'googol.' Bender's literal approach to fighting a number name is appropriately ridiculous.

jokes.text: For me, even this world is too real.
jokes.setup: Discussion about loving mathematics in reality
jokes.punchline: Cantor finds physical reality itself insufficiently abstract
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment for the historical mathematician. The extreme abstraction preference is both funny and philosophically consistent.

jokes.text: I don't care either. But it does feel like I should say thank you, Hermes.
jokes.setup: Hermes admits he doesn't really care about Professor's return
jokes.punchline: Professor reciprocates the emotional honesty while following social convention
jokes.explanation: Nice callback to Hermes's earlier meta-commentary. Both characters acknowledging the meaninglessness while participating is nicely absurd.

jokes.text: Another second-place, third-rate puppy? No, it's a paint-by-number I'm unmaking just for you.
jokes.setup: Professor dismisses Fry's art with characteristic cruelty
jokes.punchline: Fry is creating art by removing paint to reveal numbers
jokes.explanation: Excellent resolution bringing together multiple threads. Fry's thoughtful gesture and the concept of 'unmaking' art is cleverly inverted.

jokes.text: I don't know which is more beautiful, the idea or the thing itself.
jokes.setup: Professor seeing Fry's number-only painting
jokes.punchline: Philosophical resolution of his abstract vs. concrete conflict
jokes.explanation: Perfect character resolution. Professor finding synthesis between his abstract ideals and concrete reality. Philosophically satisfying.

jokes.text: I don't know which is more beautiful, the idea or the thing itself. Exquisite.
jokes.setup: Professor contemplates Fry's number art
jokes.punchline: Returns to his catchphrase while bridging abstract and physical appreciation
jokes.explanation: Perfect ending that resolves the abstract vs. physical theme. Professor's growth while maintaining his character voice is well-executed.
